step.title: Step 17
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE activates chamber to dark gray')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 21, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE changed chamber color from white (W) to dark gray (G) for rows 32-35. Row 36 remains white. Chamber is "activating" - each SPACE press converts white rows to dark gray.
- Plan: Press SPACE again to activate the remaining white row, then check if the projectile can be launched.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After the second SPACE, the chamber went back to all white (W)! The ascii shows:
- Row 32: WWWW
- Row 33: WbbW
- Row 34: WbbW
- Row 35: WWWW
- Row 36: WWWW
So the pattern is:
1. Initial state: chamber is all white (W)
2. After 1st SPACE: rows 32-35 become dark gray (G), row 36 remains white
3. After 2nd SPACE: chamber goes back to all white (W)
This is a toggle or cycle behavior! SPACE toggles the chamber between white and dark gray states.
So the chamber has two states:
- White (W): inactive
- Dark gray (G): active
When the chamber is active (dark gray), maybe the projectile can be launched or the player can move through it?
Let me try pressing SPACE once more to activate the chamber, then immediately try moving UP to see if the player can move through the active chamber.
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The first SPACE turned rows 32-35 to dark gray. The second SPACE turned everything back to white. So it's a toggle: SPACE toggles between the two states.
But what's the purpose of this toggle? Maybe when the chamber is dark gray (active), the player can move UP through it?
Let me test this: press SPACE to activate the chamber, then try UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE toggles the chamber between white (inactive) and dark gray (active). Pattern: white → dark gray → white → ... The chamber state might affect whether the player can move UP through it.
Plan: Press SPACE to activate chamber (dark gray), then immediately try UP to see if player can move through the active chamber.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Press SPACE to activate chamber, then try UP
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE activates chamber to dark gray')
action(['SPACE'])

# Verify chamber is now dark gray
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("After SPACE (chamber should be dark gray):")
for r in range(32, 37):
    row_str = ascii_lines[r]
    cols_slice = row_str[14:22] if len(row_str) > 21 else row_str
    print(f"Row {r}: '{cols_slice}'")

# Now try UP
predict(expect_change=True, note='UP should work if chamber is active')
action(['UP'])

# Check if player moved
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"\nPlayer after UP: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Check ascii again
print("\nAfter UP:")
for r in range(28, 42):
    if r < len(ascii_lines):
        row_str = ascii_lines[r]
        cols_slice = row_str[14:22] if len(row_str) > 21 else row_str
        print(f"Row {r}: '{cols_slice}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
